class_schedule.topic: Contract Law Fundamentals for Project Managers
learning_objectives: Understand the elements of a valid contract
Identify key contract law principles relevant to project management
Distinguish between different contract structures and their legal implications
readings: Garrett, Contract Management for Project Managers, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Every project manager works with contracts. Understanding contract law principles protects the project and the organization.
key_concepts: Contract elements: offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity, legality
Express vs. implied terms
Breach of contract: material breach vs. minor breach, remedies
Contract interpretation: parol evidence rule, ambiguity resolution
Statute of frauds: which contracts must be in writing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a sample contract to identify essential elements and potential interpretation issues

review.value: A breach that goes to the essence of the contract; the non-breaching party may terminate and seek damages
If the contract is integrated (final and complete), prior negotiations and oral agreements cannot modify written terms

learning_objectives: Draft key contract clauses including scope, payment, and change provisions
Evaluate risk allocation in contract terms
Negotiate balanced contract terms that protect both parties

lecture_notes.introduction: The contract is the PM's primary tool for managing vendor relationships. Understanding what each clause means and why it matters is essential.
key_concepts: Scope of work clause: clear, measurable, and complete deliverable descriptions
Payment terms: milestone-based, time-based, deliverable-based; retention provisions
Change order process: authority levels, pricing mechanisms, documentation requirements
Limitation of liability: caps on damages, consequential damages waiver
Termination clauses: termination for cause, termination for convenience, notice requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reviewing and red-lining a vendor contract, identifying favorable and unfavorable terms

review.value: Allows the buyer to terminate without cause; important flexibility but requires fair compensation for work completed and demobilization
Caps the maximum the vendor can owe; without it, a small contract could create unlimited liability exposure

learning_objectives: Monitor vendor performance against contract requirements
Implement KPI frameworks for contract performance measurement
Handle vendor underperformance using contractual remedies

lecture_notes.introduction: A contract is only as good as its enforcement. Active contract administration ensures the vendor delivers what was promised.
key_concepts: KPI framework: define, measure, report, improve performance metrics
Service Level Agreements: specific, measurable performance standards
Performance review meetings: regular cadence, data-driven discussions, action items
Cure notices: formal notification of contract breach with deadline for correction
Liquidated damages vs. actual damages: predetermined vs. proven-at-trial compensation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a vendor performance scorecard and conducting a simulated quarterly performance review

review.value: Formal written notice that the vendor is in breach; specifies the breach, required correction, and timeframe; required before termination for cause
Pre-agreed amount per day/incident of breach; must be a reasonable estimate of actual damages, not a penalty

learning_objectives: Identify common sources of contract disputes in projects
Apply the dispute resolution ladder: negotiation, mediation, arbitration, litigation
Prepare a claim package with supporting documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Disputes are inevitable in long-term contracts. How they are resolved affects cost, time, and the ongoing vendor relationship.
key_concepts: Common dispute sources: scope interpretation, change order pricing, quality disagreements, delay responsibility
Negotiation: first step, lowest cost, preserves relationship
Mediation: neutral third party facilitates agreement; non-binding
Arbitration: neutral arbitrator makes binding decision; faster than litigation
Claim package: factual narrative, contract references, supporting documentation, damage calculation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Preparing a claim package for a contractor alleging owner-caused delay on a construction project

review.value: Start with the least adversarial method; each step up costs more and takes longer; most disputes settle before arbitration
Claims succeed or fail on documentation; daily logs, emails, meeting minutes, and photos are the evidence

learning_objectives: Execute contract closure procedures ensuring all obligations are met
Conduct contract lessons learned for organizational knowledge
Apply contract management best practices to improve future procurements
readings: CIPS Guide to Contract Management (closure sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Contract closure protects both parties by ensuring all obligations are fulfilled and documented before the relationship formally ends.
key_concepts: Closure checklist: all deliverables accepted, all payments made, all disputes resolved, warranties documented
Final acceptance: formal sign-off that all contract requirements have been met
Warranty management: documenting warranty terms, tracking warranty periods, processing warranty claims
Vendor evaluation: final performance assessment feeding into organizational vendor database
Contract lessons learned: what contract terms worked, what caused disputes, what to change in future contracts
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walking through a complete contract closure process for a completed IT services engagement

review.value: Starts at formal acceptance; the vendor is responsible for defects discovered during this period at no additional cost
Feed into organizational procurement templates and standards; improves future contract quality

class_schedule.topic: Special Contract Types and Emerging Issues
learning_objectives: Manage government contracts with unique regulatory requirements
Navigate intellectual property provisions in project contracts
Address data privacy and cybersecurity in modern contracts

lecture_notes.introduction: Some contracts require specialized knowledge. Government contracts, IP-heavy technology contracts, and data-intensive contracts have unique considerations.
key_concepts: Government contracts: FAR compliance, protest procedures, small business requirements
IP provisions: work-for-hire, license-back, joint IP ownership, open-source compliance
Data privacy: GDPR, CCPA, data processing agreements, data breach notification requirements
Cybersecurity: CMMC (Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification), security requirements flow-down
SaaS contracts: subscription terms, data ownership, exit provisions, SLA commitments
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reviewing a SaaS contract for data privacy compliance and identifying necessary amendments

review.value: Default varies by jurisdiction; always specify in the contract who owns what; work-for-hire vs. licensed-back has major implications
Plan for contract exit from the start: data portability, transition support, and adequate notice periods protect the buyer

questions.question: A vendor contract states: 'Vendor shall deliver the system by March 31.' Due to an owner-caused design change in February, the vendor delivers on April 15. Has the vendor materially breached the contract? Explain your reasoning.
questions.answer: No, the vendor has not materially breached the contract because the owner caused the delay through the February design change. The owner's action likely constitutes a constructive change (an action by the owner that effectively changes the contract requirements without a formal change order). The vendor is entitled to a time extension for the owner-caused delay. To determine this: (1) assess whether the design change impacted the critical path, (2) quantify the delay caused by the change (likely the 15 days), (3) the vendor should have notified the owner of the delay impact per the contract's change/delay notification provisions. The March 31 date should be adjusted to account for the owner-caused delay.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies no material breach by vendor
Explains owner-caused delay as the reason
References constructive change and time extension

questions.question: Explain why a 'termination for convenience' clause benefits the buyer, and describe what the buyer typically owes the vendor when exercising it.
questions.answer: Termination for convenience benefits the buyer by providing flexibility to cancel the contract without proving vendor fault if the project is no longer needed, strategy changes, or budget is cut. Without it, the buyer could only terminate for cause (vendor breach), which requires evidence and is often contested. When exercised, the buyer typically owes: (1) payment for all work completed and accepted to date, (2) payment for work in progress at a fair valuation, (3) reasonable demobilization costs (returning materials, releasing subcontractors), (4) profit on completed work (not on unperformed work). The vendor is NOT entitled to anticipated profit on the remaining unperformed portion of the contract.

rubric.key: Explains the flexibility benefit to the buyer
Lists at least 3 categories of payment owed
Notes vendor does NOT get profit on unperformed work

questions.question: A vendor's SLA requires 99.9% uptime for a production system. In March, actual uptime was 99.5%. The contract specifies liquidated damages of $5,000 per 0.1% below the SLA. Calculate the liquidated damages and explain the process the PM should follow.
questions.answer: Shortfall = 99.9% - 99.5% = 0.4%. Liquidated damages = 0.4% / 0.1% x $5,000 = 4 x $5,000 = $20,000. Process: (1) Document the actual uptime with monitoring data and timestamps of outages, (2) Send formal notice to the vendor citing the specific SLA clause, the measured performance, and the calculated liquidated damages, (3) Deduct the $20,000 from the next invoice (if the contract allows) or demand payment, (4) Request a root cause analysis and corrective action plan from the vendor, (5) Schedule a performance review meeting to discuss prevention measures.

rubric.key: Documents with monitoring data and sends formal notice
Requests corrective action, not just payment

questions.question: Describe the key differences between mediation and arbitration as contract dispute resolution methods, and explain why most contracts include both in a stepped resolution clause.
questions.answer: Mediation: a neutral mediator facilitates negotiation between parties, but the mediator cannot impose a decision; any agreement is voluntary. Advantages: preserves relationship, parties control the outcome, faster, cheaper. Arbitration: a neutral arbitrator hears evidence and makes a binding decision that is enforceable in court. Advantages: finality, expertise (arbitrator can be industry expert), faster than litigation. Stepped clauses include both because: (1) mediation resolves most disputes at low cost while preserving the relationship, (2) arbitration provides a backstop for disputes that mediation cannot resolve, (3) the sequence ensures the less adversarial method is tried first, (4) knowing arbitration is the next step incentivizes good-faith mediation.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes binding vs. non-binding nature

major_assignments.title: Contract Package Development and Administration Plan
major_assignments.description: Draft key contract sections for a project procurement, develop a contract administration plan with KPIs, and create a dispute resolution framework.
objectives: Draft scope of work, payment, change, and termination clauses
Design a contract KPI framework with SLAs and liquidated damages
Create a dispute resolution framework with stepped escalation
deliverables: Draft contract sections: scope, payment, change, liability, and termination (5-6 pages)
Contract administration plan with KPI framework and SLAs (3-4 pages)
Dispute resolution framework with procedures and templates (2-3 pages)
Contract closure checklist with final evaluation criteria (2 pages)

criteria: All required clauses present and professionally drafted
Risk allocation is balanced and intentional
Terms are clear, specific, and enforceable
KPIs are measurable, achievable, and aligned with contract objectives
SLAs have specific targets and consequences
Reporting cadence and review process defined
Stepped approach from negotiation through arbitration
Timelines and procedures for each step
Comprehensive checklist covering all closure activities

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on contract law, drafting, performance, and disputes

contract_project.description: Contract package development and administration plan

midterm_examination.description: Contract law fundamentals and clause drafting

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all contract management topics

class_participation.description: Participation in contract review exercises, negotiation simulations, and dispute resolution role-plays

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Negotiation simulations require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Negotiation exercises are collaborative. The contract package assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All contract language must be original drafting. Using unmodified template language without understanding is not acceptable.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Word processor for contract drafting. Spreadsheet for KPI tracking. No specialized legal software required.
